reasoning: I will provide a concise, patient-friendly prevention summary in fixed, gold-target style: one core statement, 5 short bullets (cause/mechanism, symptoms/implications, management options, caveats, practical next step), and a closing note.
output:
Preventing atherosclerosis means reducing risk factors to slow or prevent plaque buildup in arteries.

- Cause/Mechanism: Artery walls are damaged by risk factors (high LDL cholesterol, high blood pressure, smoking, diabetes, obesity, inactivity), which can lead to plaque formation over time.
- Symptoms/Implications: Many people have no symptoms early; prevention aims to lower the risk of heart attack and stroke.
- Management Options: Heart-healthy lifestyle is first: eat lots of fruits/vegetables/whole grains, lean proteins and fish; limit saturated fat and salt; maintain a healthy weight; be physically active (about 2.5 hours moderate activity per week); quit smoking; limit alcohol. Medications (e.g., statins) may be used if your risk is high.
- caveats/Special Situations: Some factors you can’t change (age, family history); risk rises with more factors and can start in childhood; responses to prevention vary by person.
- Practical Next Step: Talk with your doctor about your personal risk, get recommended tests (lipids, blood pressure, blood sugar), and start a tailored prevention plan; seek urgent care if you have chest pain or signs of stroke.

This information is general; individual risk and decisions depend on specific circumstances and should be discussed with a qualified professional.
